question_html: A charged sphere of radius r has surface charge density. The electric field on its surface is E. If the radius of the sphere is doubled, keeping charge density the same, the ratio of the electric field on the old sphere to that on the new sphere will be:
question_text: A charged sphere of radius r has surface charge density. The electric field on its surface is E. If the radius of the sphere is doubled, keeping charge density the same, the ratio of the electric field on the old sphere to that on the new sphere will be:

options.explanation: Given that the electric field E on the surface of a charged sphere is determined by the surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} , the formula is: {tex}E=\frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} When the radius of the sphere is doubled, but the surface charge density remains the same, the electric field on the surface of the sphere does not change, because it still depends solely on {tex}\sigma{/tex} and not on the radius. Therefore, for both the original sphere and the new sphere: {tex} E_{\text {old }} \downarrow \nabla_{\text {new }}=\frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} Thus, the ratio of the electric field on the old sphere to that on the new sphere is: {tex} \frac{E_{\text {old }}}{E_{\text {new }}}=1{/tex}

explanation: Given that the electric field E on the surface of a charged sphere is determined by the surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} , the formula is: {tex}E=\frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} When the radius of the sphere is doubled, but the surface charge density remains the same, the electric field on the surface of the sphere does not change, because it still depends solely on {tex}\sigma{/tex} and not on the radius. Therefore, for both the original sphere and the new sphere: {tex} E_{\text {old }} \downarrow \nabla_{\text {new }}=\frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0}{/tex} Thus, the ratio of the electric field on the old sphere to that on the new sphere is: {tex} \frac{E_{\text {old }}}{E_{\text {new }}}=1{/tex}

question_html: Four point charges qA = 2{tex}\mu{/tex}C, qB = -5{tex}\mu{/tex}C, qC = 2{tex}\mu{/tex}C and qD = -5{tex}\mu{/tex}C are located at the corners of a square ABCD of side 10 cm. What is the force on a charge of 1{tex}\mu{/tex}C placed at the centre of the square?
question_text: Four point charges q A = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, q B = -5 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, q C = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and q D = -5 {tex}\mu{/tex} C are located at the corners of a square ABCD of side 10 cm. What is the force on a charge of 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C placed at the centre of the square?
explanation: Suppose a square ABCD with each side of 10 cm and centre O. At the centre, the charge of 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C is placed. Given: q A = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, q B = -5 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, q C = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and q D = -5 {tex}\mu{/tex} C As q A = q C , the charge of 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C experiences equal and opposite forces F A and F C due to charges q A and q C . At the same time, the charge 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C experiences equal and opposite forces F B and F D due to equal charges q B and q D at B and D. Thus, the net force on charge of 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C due to the given charges is zero.
solution: Suppose a square ABCD with each side of 10 cm and centre O. At the centre, the charge of 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C is placed. Given: q A = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, q B = -5 {tex}\mu{/tex} C, q C = 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and q D = -5 {tex}\mu{/tex} C As q A = q C , the charge of 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C experiences equal and opposite forces F A and F C due to charges q A and q C . At the same time, the charge 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C experiences equal and opposite forces F B and F D due to equal charges q B and q D at B and D. Thus, the net force on charge of 1 {tex}\mu{/tex} C due to the given charges is zero.
solution_html:
Suppose a square ABCD with each side of 10 cm and centre O. At the centre, the charge of 1{tex}\mu{/tex}C  is placed.

Given: qA = 2{tex}\mu{/tex}C, qB = -5{tex}\mu{/tex}C, qC = 2{tex}\mu{/tex}C and qD = -5{tex}\mu{/tex}C
As qA = qC, the charge of 1{tex}\mu{/tex}C experiences equal and opposite forces FA and FC due to charges qA and qC.
At the same time, the charge 1{tex}\mu{/tex}C experiences equal and opposite forces FB and FD due to equal charges qB and qD at B and D.
Thus, the net force on charge of 1{tex}\mu{/tex}C due to the given charges is zero.

question_html:
A polythene piece rubbed with wool is found to have a negative charge of 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7C.

  1. Estimate the number of electrons transferred (from which to which)?
  2. Is there a transfer of mass from wool to polythene?
question_text: A polythene piece rubbed with wool is found to have a negative charge of 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 C. Estimate the number of electrons transferred (from which to which)? Is there a transfer of mass from wool to polythene?
explanation: When polythene is rubbed against wool, a number of electrons get transferred from wool to polythene. Hence, wool becomes positively charged and polythene becomes negatively charged. Amount of charge on the polythene piece, q = −3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −7 C Amount of charge on an electron, e = −1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −19 C Number of electrons transferred from wool to polythene = n n can be calculated using the relation, q = ne n = {tex}\frac{q}{e}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-3 \times 10^{-7}}{-1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 1.87 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 12 Therefore, the number of electrons transferred from wool to polythene is 1.87 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 12 . Yes. There is a transfer of mass occur. This is because an electron has mass, as we know m e = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −3 kg Total mass transferred from wool to polythene, m = me {tex}\times{/tex} n = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −31 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.87 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 12 = 1.706 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −18 kg Hence, a negligible amount of mass is transferred from wool to polythene.
solution: When polythene is rubbed against wool, a number of electrons get transferred from wool to polythene. Hence, wool becomes positively charged and polythene becomes negatively charged. Amount of charge on the polythene piece, q = −3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −7 C Amount of charge on an electron, e = −1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −19 C Number of electrons transferred from wool to polythene = n n can be calculated using the relation, q = ne n = {tex}\frac{q}{e}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-3 \times 10^{-7}}{-1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 1.87 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 12 Therefore, the number of electrons transferred from wool to polythene is 1.87 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 12 . Yes. There is a transfer of mass occur. This is because an electron has mass, as we know m e = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −3 kg Total mass transferred from wool to polythene, m = me {tex}\times{/tex} n = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −31 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.87 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 12 = 1.706 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 −18 kg Hence, a negligible amount of mass is transferred from wool to polythene.
solution_html:
1. When polythene is rubbed against wool, a number of electrons get transferred from wool to polythene. Hence, wool becomes positively charged and polythene becomes negatively charged.
    Amount of charge on the polythene piece, q = −3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−7 C
    Amount of charge on an electron, e = −1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−19 C
    Number of electrons transferred from wool to polythene = n
    n can be calculated using the relation, q = ne
    n = {tex}\frac{q}{e}{/tex} 
    = {tex}\frac{-3 \times 10^{-7}}{-1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} 
    = 1.87 {tex}\times{/tex} 1012 
    Therefore, the number of electrons transferred from wool to polythene is 1.87 {tex}\times{/tex} 1012.
  2. Yes. There is a transfer of mass occur. This is because an electron has mass, as we know
    me = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−3 kg Total mass transferred from wool to polythene,
    m = me {tex}\times{/tex} n
    = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−31 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.87{tex}\times{/tex} 1012 = 1.706 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−18 kg
    Hence, a negligible amount of mass is transferred from wool to polythene.

question_html: Using Gauss' law, obtain the expression for the electric field due to uniformly charged spherical shell of radius R at a point outside the shell. Draw a graph showing the variation of electric field with r, for r > R and r < R.
question_text: Using Gauss' law, obtain the expression for the electric field due to uniformly charged spherical shell of radius R at a point outside the shell. Draw a graph showing the variation of electric field with r, for r > R and r < R.
explanation: Considering a Gaussian surface in the form of a sphere at radius r, the electric field has the same magnitude at every point of the sphere and is directed outward. Let us consider charge +q be uniformly distributed over a spherical shell of radius R. 'E' at any point is radially outward (if charge q is positive) and has same magnitude at all points which lie at the same distance r from centre of spherical shell such that r > R. Therefore, Gaussian surface is concentric sphere of radius r such that r > R. By Gauss' theorem {tex}\oint \mathbf { E } \cdot d \mathbf { S } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \Rightarrow \oint E d S \cos 0 ^ { \circ } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} E and dS are along the same direction] {tex}E \cdot \oint d S = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} Magnitude of E is same at every point on Gaussian surface] {tex}E \times 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad E = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Now, graph is shown below. Variation of E with r for a spherical shell in which charge q is distributed over its surface which shows that electric field inside a spherical shell is zero.

Therefore, Gaussian surface is concentric sphere of radius r such that r > R. Variation of E with r for a spherical shell in which charge q is distributed over its surface which shows that electric field inside a spherical shell is zero.
solution_html:
Considering a Gaussian surface in the form of a sphere at radius r, the electric field has the same magnitude at every point of the sphere and is directed outward. Let us consider charge +q be uniformly distributed over a spherical shell of radius R.
'E' at any point is radially outward (if charge q is positive) and has same magnitude at all points which lie at the same distance r from centre of spherical shell such that r > R.
Therefore, Gaussian surface is concentric sphere of radius r such that r > R.

By Gauss' theorem
{tex}\oint \mathbf { E } \cdot d \mathbf { S } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \Rightarrow \oint E d S \cos 0 ^ { \circ } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex}
[{tex}\because {/tex} E and dS are along the same direction]
{tex}E \cdot \oint d S = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} [{tex}\because {/tex} Magnitude of E is same at every point on Gaussian surface]
{tex}E \times 4 \pi r ^ { 2 } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad E = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q } { r ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
Now, graph is shown below.

question_text: Two electric field lines cannot cross each other. Also, they cannot form closed loops. Give reasons. A particle of charge 2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and mass 1.6 g is moving with a velocity 4 {tex}\hat {i}{/tex} ms -1 . Consider a uniform electric field {tex}E = 3 \times {10^3}\hat i\;N/C{/tex}.

  1. What is the flux of this field through a square of 10 cm on a side whose plane is parallel to the yz plane?
  2. What is the flux through the same square if the normal to its plane makes a 60° angle with the x-axis?
question_text: Consider a uniform electric field {tex}E = 3 \times {10^3}\hat i\;N/C{/tex} . What is the flux of this field through a square of 10 cm on a side whose plane is parallel to the yz plane? What is the flux through the same square if the normal to its plane makes a 60° angle with the x-axis?
explanation: Given, {tex}\overrightarrow E = 3 \times {10^3}\hat i\;N{C^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}\Delta S{/tex} (Area of the square) {tex} = 10 \times 10 = 100c{m^2} = {10^{ - 2}}{m^{ 2}}{/tex} The area of a surface can be represented as a vector along normal to the surface. Since normal to the square is along x-axis, we have {tex}\Delta \overrightarrow S = {10^{ - 2}}\hat i\;{m^{ 2}}{/tex} Electric flux through the square is given by :- {tex}\phi = \overrightarrow E \cdot \overrightarrow {\Delta S} {/tex} = {tex}(3 \times {10^3}\hat i) \cdot ({10^{ - 2}}\hat i){/tex} {tex} = 30N{m^2}{C^{ - 1}}{/tex} Given, the angle between area vector and the electric field is 60°. Therefore, {tex}\phi = \overrightarrow E \cdot \Delta \overrightarrow S {/tex} {tex} = E \cdot \Delta S\;\cos 60^\circ {/tex} {tex} = 3 \times {10^3} \times {10^{ - 2}} \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex} = 15N{m^2}{C^{ - 1}}{/tex}

Given, {tex}\overrightarrow E = 3 \times {10^3}\hat i\;N{C^{ - 1}}{/tex}

  1. {tex}\Delta S{/tex} (Area of the square) {tex} = 10 \times 10 = 100c{m^2} = {10^{ - 2}}{m^{ 2}}{/tex}
    The area of a surface can be represented as a vector along normal to the surface. Since normal to the square is along x-axis, we have
    {tex}\Delta \overrightarrow S = {10^{ - 2}}\hat i\;{m^{ 2}}{/tex}

    Electric flux through the square is given by :-
    {tex}\phi = \overrightarrow E \cdot \overrightarrow {\Delta S} {/tex}
    = {tex}(3 \times {10^3}\hat i) \cdot ({10^{ - 2}}\hat i){/tex}
    {tex} = 30N{m^2}{C^{ - 1}}{/tex}
  2. Given, the angle between area vector and the electric field is 60°. Therefore,

question_html: A point charge {tex} + 10\mu C{/tex} is a distance 5 cm directly above the center of a square of side 10 cm, as shown in figure. What is the magnitude of the electric flux through the square? (Hint: Think of the square as one face of a cube with edge 10 cm)
question_text: A point charge {tex} + 10\mu C{/tex} is a distance 5 cm directly above the center of a square of side 10 cm, as shown in figure. What is the magnitude of the electric flux through the square? (Hint: Think of the square as one face of a cube with edge 10 cm)
explanation: Let us assume that the charge {tex}q = \pm 10\mu C = { \pm10^{ - 5}}C{/tex} is placed at a distance of 5 cm from the square ABCD of each side 10 cm. The square ABCD can be considered as one of the six faces of a cubic Gaussian surface of each side 10 cm. Now the total electric flux through the faces of the cube as per Gaussian theorem {tex}\phi = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} Therefore, the total electric flux through the square ABCD will be {tex}{\phi _E} = \frac{1}{6} \times \phi = \frac{1}{6} \times \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{6} \times \frac{{{{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{8.854 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}}{/tex} {tex} = 1.88 \times {10^5}{/tex} Nm 2 C -1 Therefore, electric flux through the square is 1.88 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 Nm 2 C -1 .

Now the total electric flux through the faces of the cube as per Gaussian theorem {tex}\phi = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} Therefore, the total electric flux through the square ABCD will be {tex}{\phi _E} = \frac{1}{6} \times \phi = \frac{1}{6} \times \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{6} \times \frac{{{{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{8.854 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}}{/tex} {tex} = 1.88 \times {10^5}{/tex} Nm 2 C -1 Therefore, electric flux through the square is 1.88 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 Nm 2 C -1 .
solution_html:
Let us assume that the charge {tex}q = \pm 10\mu C = { \pm10^{ - 5}}C{/tex}  is placed at a distance of 5 cm from the square ABCD of each side 10 cm. The square ABCD can be considered as one of the six faces of a cubic Gaussian surface of each side 10 cm.
Now the total electric flux through the faces of the cube as per Gaussian theorem
{tex}\phi = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex}
Therefore, the total electric flux through the square ABCD will be

{tex}{\phi _E} = \frac{1}{6} \times \phi = \frac{1}{6} \times \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{1}{6} \times \frac{{{{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{8.854 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}}{/tex}
{tex} = 1.88 \times {10^5}{/tex} Nm2C-1
Therefore, electric flux through the square is 1.88 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 Nm2C-1.

question_html: Two fixed point charges +4e and +e units are separated by a distance a. Where should a third charge q be placed for it to be in equilibrium?
question_text: Two fixed point charges +4e and +e units are separated by a distance a. Where should a third charge q be placed for it to be in equilibrium?
explanation: Suppose the three charges are placed as shown in Fig. Let the charge q be positive. For the equilibrium of charge + q, we must have Force of repulsion F 1 between + 4e and + q = Force of repulsion F 2 between + e and + q or {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{4 e \times q}{x^{2}}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{e \times q}{(a-x)^{2}}{/tex} or 4 (a - x) 2 = x 2 or 2 (a - x) = {tex}\pm{/tex} x {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{2 a}{3}{/tex} or 2a As the charge q is placed between + 4e and + e, so only x = {tex}\frac {2a}3{/tex} is possible. Hence for equilibrium, the charge q must be placed at a distance {tex}\frac {2a}3{/tex} from the charge + 4e. We have considered the charge q to be positive. If we displace it slightly towards charge e, from the equilibrium position, then F 1 will decrease and F 2 will increase and a net force (F 2 - F 1 ) will act on q towards left i.e., towards the equilibrium position. Hence the equilibrium of positive q is stable. Now if we take charge q to be negative, the forces F 1 and F 2 will be attractive, as shown in Fig. The charge - q will still be in equilibrium at x = {tex}\frac {2a}3{/tex} . However, if we displace charge - q slightly towards the right, then F 1 force will decrease as distance increases and F 2 will increase because distance decreases. A net force (F 2 - F 1 ) will act on - q towards the right i.e., away from the equilibrium position. So it will be in in unstable equilibrium position.

question_html: Write Coulomb's law in vector form. What is the importance of expressing it in vector form?
question_text: Write Coulomb's law in vector form. What is the importance of expressing it in vector form?
explanation: Coulomb's law in vector form: As shown in fig., consider two positive point charges q 1 and q 2 placed in vacuum at distance r from each other. They repel each other. In vector form, Coulomb's law may be expressed as {tex}\vec{F}_{21}{/tex} = Force on charge q 2 due to q 1 = {tex}\frac{1}{4\pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}} \hat{r}_{12}{/tex} where {tex}\hat{r}_{12}=\frac{\overrightarrow{r_{12}}}{r}{/tex} , is a unit vector in the direction from q 1 to q 2 Similarly, {tex}\vec{F}_{12}{/tex} = Force on charge q 1 due to q 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}} \hat{r}_{21}{/tex} where {tex}\hat{r}_{21}=\frac{\vec{r}_{21}}{r}{/tex} , is a unit vector in the direction from q 2 to q 1 . The coloumbian forces between unlike charges (q 1 q 2 < 0) are attractive, as shown in Fig. Importance of vector form. The vector form of coulomb's law gives the following additional information : As {tex}\hat{r}_{21}=-\hat{r}_{12}{/tex} , therefore {tex}\vec{F}_{21}=-\vec{F}_{12}{/tex} This means that the two charges exert equal and opposite forces on each other. So Coulombian forces obey Newton's third law of motion. As the Coulombian forces act along {tex}\vec{F}_{12}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{F}_{21}{/tex} , i.e., along the line joining the centres of two charges, so they are central forces.

Coulomb's law in vector form:  As shown in fig., consider two positive point charges q1 and q2 placed in vacuum at distance r from each other. They repel each other.

In vector form, Coulomb's law may be expressed as {tex}\vec{F}_{21}{/tex} = Force on charge q2 due to q1
= {tex}\frac{1}{4\pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}} \hat{r}_{12}{/tex} where {tex}\hat{r}_{12}=\frac{\overrightarrow{r_{12}}}{r}{/tex}, is a unit vector in the direction from q1 to q2
Similarly, {tex}\vec{F}_{12}{/tex} = Force on charge q1 due to q2
= {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r^{2}} \hat{r}_{21}{/tex} where {tex}\hat{r}_{21}=\frac{\vec{r}_{21}}{r}{/tex}, is a unit vector in the direction from q2 to q1.
The coloumbian forces between unlike charges (q1q2 < 0) are attractive, as shown in Fig.

Importance of vector form. The vector form of coulomb's law gives the following additional information :

  1. As {tex}\hat{r}_{21}=-\hat{r}_{12}{/tex}, therefore {tex}\vec{F}_{21}=-\vec{F}_{12}{/tex}
    This means that the two charges exert equal and opposite forces on each other. So Coulombian forces obey Newton's third law of motion.
  2. As the Coulombian forces act along {tex}\vec{F}_{12}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{F}_{21}{/tex}, i.e., along the line joining the centres of two charges, so they are central forces.

question_text: Two charges q and -3q are placed fixed on x-axis separated by distance d . Where should a third charge 2q be placed such that it will not experience any force?
explanation: If we place the third charge 2q between A and B the direction of the force on 2q due to A and B on C will be same. So the net force cannot be zero, so the charge q cannot be placed between A and B. If 2q is placed the right side of A then r AC > r BC as q A < q B . So F CA < F CB always as the direction of F CA is towards right and F CB is left so, F CA + F CB {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 we can not obtain required condition. Now consider 2q at the C to left of q at distance x from q. Force on 2q at C (left of q) is in opposite direction so net force will be zero if magnitude is equal. So, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} F CA + F CB = 0 or F CA = - F CB {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{K q_{C} q_{A}}{r^{2} C A}=\frac{-K q_{C} q_{B}}{r^{2} C B}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 q q}{x^{2}}=\frac{-2 q(-3 q)}{(x+d)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 q^{2}}{x^{2}}=\frac{6 q^{2}}{(x+d)^{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x^{2}}=\frac{3}{(x+d)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x 2 = x 2 + d 2 + 2xd {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 2 - 2xd - d 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{+2 d \pm \sqrt{(-2 d)^{2}-4.2 \cdot\left(-d^{2}\right)}}{2.2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{+2 d \pm \sqrt{4 d^{2}+8 d^{2}}}{4}=\frac{2 d \pm 2 d \sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{d(1 \pm \sqrt{3})}{2}{/tex} So, {tex}x=\frac{d}{2}(1+\sqrt{3}){/tex} m to the left of q, it will not experience any force

1. If we place the third charge 2q between A and B the direction of the force on 2q due to A and B on C will be same.
    So the net force cannot be zero, so the charge q cannot be placed between A and B.
  2. If 2q is placed the right side of A then rAC > rBC as qA < qB. So FCA < FCB  always as the direction of FCA is towards right and FCB is left so, FCA + FCB  {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 we can not obtain required condition.
  3. Now consider 2q at the C to left of q at distance x from q.
    Force on 2q at C (left of q) is in opposite direction so net force will be zero if magnitude is equal. So,
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} FCA + FCB = 0 or FCA = - FCB 
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{K q_{C} q_{A}}{r^{2} C A}=\frac{-K q_{C} q_{B}}{r^{2} C B}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 q q}{x^{2}}=\frac{-2 q(-3 q)}{(x+d)^{2}}{/tex}

What is the electric field

  1. inside the sphere?
  2. just outside the sphere?

question_text: A spherical conductor of radius 12 cm has a charge of {tex}1.6 \times {10^{ - 7}}C{/tex} distributed uniformly on its surface. What is the electric field inside the sphere? just outside the sphere? at a point 18 cm from the centre of the sphere?
explanation: Given, {tex}q = 1.6 \times {10^{ - 7}}C{/tex} r = 0.12 m Electric field is zero inside the sphere because charge reside on outer surface of conductor. question_html:
S1 and S2 are two concentric spheres enclosing charges Q and 2Q respectively as shown in Fig.

  1. What is the ratio of the electric flux through S1 and S2?
  2. How will the electric flux through the sphere S1, change, if a medium of dielectric constant k is introduced in the space inside S1 in place of air?
  3. How will the electric flux through sphere S1 change, if a medium of dielectric constant k is introduced in the space inside S2, in place of air?
question_text: S 1 and S 2 are two concentric spheres enclosing charges Q and 2Q respectively as shown in Fig. What is the ratio of the electric flux through S 1 and S 2 ? How will the electric flux through the sphere S 1 , change, if a medium of dielectric constant k is introduced in the space inside S 1 in place of air? How will the electric flux through sphere S 1 change, if a medium of dielectric constant k is introduced in the space inside S 2 , in place of air?
explanation: By Gauss's Theorem, Flux through S 1 is {tex}\phi_{1}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Flux through S 2 is {tex}\phi_{2}=\frac{2 Q+Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{3 Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Ratio of electric flux through S 1 and S 2 is {tex}\frac{\phi_{1}}{\phi_{2}}=\frac{Q / \varepsilon_{0}}{3 Q / \varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} = 1 : 3 If a medium of dielectric constant k is introduced in the space inside S 1 , then flux through S 1 becomes {tex}\phi_{1}^{\prime}=\oint \vec{E}^{\prime} \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}=\oint \frac{\vec{E}}{\kappa} \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}=\frac{1}{\kappa} \oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}=\frac{1}{\kappa} \cdot \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} The flux through S 1 does not change with the introduction of the dielectric medium inside the sphere S 2 .
solution: By Gauss's Theorem, Flux through S 1 is {tex}\phi_{1}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Flux through S 2 is {tex}\phi_{2}=\frac{2 Q+Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{3 Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Ratio of electric flux through S 1 and S 2 is {tex}\frac{\phi_{1}}{\phi_{2}}=\frac{Q / \varepsilon_{0}}{3 Q / \varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} = 1 : 3 If a medium of dielectric constant k is introduced in the space inside S 1 , then flux through S 1 becomes {tex}\phi_{1}^{\prime}=\oint \vec{E}^{\prime} \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}=\oint \frac{\vec{E}}{\kappa} \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}=\frac{1}{\kappa} \oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}=\frac{1}{\kappa} \cdot \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} The flux through S 1 does not change with the introduction of the dielectric medium inside the sphere S 2 .
solution_html:
1. By Gauss's Theorem,
    Flux through S1 is {tex}\phi_{1}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    Flux through S2 is {tex}\phi_{2}=\frac{2 Q+Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{3 Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    Ratio of electric flux through S1 and S2 is
    {tex}\frac{\phi_{1}}{\phi_{2}}=\frac{Q / \varepsilon_{0}}{3 Q / \varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} = 1 : 3
  2. If a medium of dielectric constant k is introduced in the space inside S1, then flux through S1 becomes
    {tex}\phi_{1}^{\prime}=\oint \vec{E}^{\prime} \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}=\oint \frac{\vec{E}}{\kappa} \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}=\frac{1}{\kappa} \oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d S}=\frac{1}{\kappa} \cdot \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
  3. The flux through S1 does not change with the introduction of the dielectric medium inside the sphere S2.

question_text: Two infinitely large plane thin parallel sheets having surface charge densities {tex}\sigma _ { 1 }{/tex} and {tex}\sigma _ { 2 } \left( \sigma _ { 1 } > \sigma _ { 2 } \right){/tex} are shown in the figure. explanation: According to the figure, A and B are two thin plane parallel sheets of charge having uniform densities {tex}\sigma _ { 1 }{/tex} and {tex}\sigma _ { 2 }{/tex} with {tex}\sigma _ { 1 } > \sigma _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\phi = E \times{/tex} area of the end faces of the cylinder {tex}E \times 2 A = \frac { \sigma A } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \Rightarrow E = \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} In region II - The electric field due to the sheet of charge A having surface charge density 'σ 1 ' will be from left to right (along the positive direction) and that due to the sheet of charge B having surface charge density 'σ 2 ' will be from right to left (along the negative direction) Therefore, in region II, we have electric field opposing each other. {tex}E = \frac { \sigma _ { 1 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } + \left( - \frac { \sigma _ { 2 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad E = \frac { 1 } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left( \sigma _ { 1 } - \sigma _ { 2 } \right){/tex} (along positive direction i.e towards right side) In region III - The electric fields due to both the charged sheets will be from left to right, i.e. along the positive direction. Therefore, in region III, we have electric fields supporting each other {tex}E = \frac { \sigma _ { 1 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } + \frac { \sigma _ { 2 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} {tex}\mathbf { E } = \frac { 1 } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left( \sigma _ { 1 } + \sigma _ { 2 } \right){/tex} (along positive direction i.e towards right side)
solution: According to the figure, A and B are two thin plane parallel sheets of charge having uniform densities {tex}\sigma _ { 1 }{/tex} and {tex}\sigma _ { 2 }{/tex} with {tex}\sigma _ { 1 } > \sigma _ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\phi = E \times{/tex} area of the end faces of the cylinder {tex}E \times 2 A = \frac { \sigma A } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \Rightarrow E = \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} In region II - The electric field due to the sheet of charge A having surface charge density 'σ 1 ' will be from left to right (along the positive direction) and that due to the sheet of charge B having surface charge density 'σ 2 ' will be from right to left (along the negative direction) Therefore, in region II, we have electric field opposing each other. {tex}E = \frac { \sigma _ { 1 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } + \left( - \frac { \sigma _ { 2 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad E = \frac { 1 } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left( \sigma _ { 1 } - \sigma _ { 2 } \right){/tex} (along positive direction i.e towards right side) In region III - The electric fields due to both the charged sheets will be from left to right, i.e. along the positive direction. Therefore, in region III, we have electric fields supporting each other {tex}E = \frac { \sigma _ { 1 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } + \frac { \sigma _ { 2 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} {tex}\mathbf { E } = \frac { 1 } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left( \sigma _ { 1 } + \sigma _ { 2 } \right){/tex} (along positive direction i.e towards right side)
solution_html:
According to the figure, A and B are two thin plane parallel sheets of charge having uniform densities {tex}\sigma _ { 1 }{/tex} and {tex}\sigma _ { 2 }{/tex}with {tex}\sigma _ { 1 } > \sigma _ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\phi = E \times{/tex} area of the end faces of the cylinder
{tex}E \times 2 A = \frac { \sigma A } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \Rightarrow E = \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex}

In region II -
The electric field due to the sheet of charge A having surface charge density 'σ1'  will be from left to right (along the positive direction) and that due to the sheet of charge B having surface charge density 'σ2' will be from right to left (along the negative direction) Therefore, in region II, we have electric field opposing each other.
{tex}E = \frac { \sigma _ { 1 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } + \left( - \frac { \sigma _ { 2 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad E = \frac { 1 } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left( \sigma _ { 1 } - \sigma _ { 2 } \right){/tex} (along positive direction i.e towards right side)
In region III -
The electric fields due to both the charged sheets will be from left to right, i.e. along the positive direction. Therefore, in region III, we have electric fields supporting each other
{tex}E = \frac { \sigma _ { 1 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } + \frac { \sigma _ { 2 } } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex}
{tex}\mathbf { E } = \frac { 1 } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left( \sigma _ { 1 } + \sigma _ { 2 } \right){/tex}(along positive direction i.e towards right side)

question_html:
1. An electric dipole is kept first to the left and then to the right of a negatively charged infinite plane sheet having a uniform surface charge density. The arrows p1 and p2 show the directions of its electric dipole moments in the two cases.

    Identify for each case, whether the dipole is in stable or unstable equilibrium. Justify each answer
  2. Next, the dipole is kept in a similar way (as shown), near an infinitely long straight wire having uniform negative linear charge density.

    Will the dipole be in equilibrium at these two positions? Justify your answer.
question_text: An electric dipole is kept first to the left and then to the right of a negatively charged infinite plane sheet having a uniform surface charge density. The arrows p 1 and p 2 show the directions of its electric dipole moments in the two cases. Identify for each case, whether the dipole is in stable or unstable equilibrium. Justify each answer Next, the dipole is kept in a similar way (as shown), near an infinitely long straight wire having uniform negative linear charge density. Will the dipole be in equilibrium at these two positions? Justify your answer.
explanation: p 1 : stable equilibrium p 2 : unstable equilibrium The electric field, on either side, is directed towards the negatively charged sheet and its magnitude is independent of the distance of the field point from the sheet. For position, p 1 dipole moment and electric field are parallel For position P 2 they are anti-parallel. In the case of P 1 dipole makes 180 o angle with electric field hence in the stable equilibrium whereas in case P 2 dipole makes 0 o angle with the electric field hence unstable. The dipole will not be in equilibrium in any of the two positions. The electric field due to an infinite straight charged wire is non-uniform (E {tex}\alpha \frac 1r{/tex} ). Hence, there will be a net non-zero force on the dipole in each case.

In the case of P 1 dipole makes 180 o angle with electric field hence in the stable equilibrium whereas in case P 2 dipole makes 0 o angle with the electric field hence unstable. Hence, there will be a net non-zero force on the dipole in each case.

  1. net flux through the cylinder.
  2. charge enclosed by the cylinder.
question_text: A hollow cylindrical box of length 1 m and area of cross-section 25 cm 2 is placed in a three-dimensional coordinate system as shown in the figure. The electric field in the region is given by {tex}E = 50 x \hat { i }{/tex} , where E is in NC -1 and x is in metre. Find net flux through the cylinder. charge enclosed by the cylinder.
explanation: Given, {tex}E = 50 x \hat i{/tex} and {tex}\Delta S = 25 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 } = 25 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex} As the electric field is only along the X-axis, so flux will pass only through the cross-section of the cylinder. Magnitude of electric field at cross-section A: {tex}E _ { A } = 50 \times 1 = 50 \mathrm { NC } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} (because Distance of first face from the origin is x = 1m) Magnitude of electric field at cross-section B: {tex}E _ { B } = 50 \times 2 = 100 \mathrm { NC } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} ( because Distance of second face from origin is x = 1 + 1 = 2m) The corresponding electric fluxes are {tex}\phi _ { A } = E _ { A } \cdot \Delta S = 50 \times 25 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \times \cos 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}= - 0.125 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} and {tex}\phi _ { B } = E _ { B } \cdot \Delta S = 100 \times 25 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \times \cos 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex} {tex}= 0.25 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} So, the net flux through the cylinder, {tex}\phi = \phi _ { A } + \phi _ { B } =- 0.125 + 0.25 = 0.125 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex} Using Gauss's law, {tex}\phi = \oint \mathbf { E } \cdot d \mathbf { l } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \Rightarrow \ 0.125= \frac { q } { 8.85 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad q =8.85 \times 0.125 \times 10 ^ { - 12 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad q = 1.1 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \mathrm { C }{/tex} So, the charge enclosed by the cylinder is {tex}1.1 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \mathrm { C }{/tex} .

solution_html:
Given, {tex}E = 50 x \hat i{/tex}
and {tex}\Delta S = 25 \mathrm { cm } ^ { 2 } = 25 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex}

As the electric field is only along the X-axis, so flux will pass only through the cross-section of the cylinder.
Magnitude of electric field at cross-section A:
{tex}E _ { A } = 50 \times 1 = 50 \mathrm { NC } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}  (because Distance of first face from the origin is x = 1m)
Magnitude of electric field at cross-section B:
{tex}E _ { B } = 50 \times 2 = 100 \mathrm { NC } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}  ( because Distance of second face from origin is x = 1 + 1 = 2m)
The corresponding electric fluxes are
{tex}\phi _ { A } = E _ { A } \cdot \Delta S = 50 \times 25 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \times \cos 180 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}= - 0.125 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}
and  {tex}\phi _ { B } = E _ { B } \cdot \Delta S = 100 \times 25 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \times \cos 0 ^ { \circ }{/tex}
{tex}= 0.25 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}

  1. So, the net flux through the cylinder,
    {tex}\phi = \phi _ { A } + \phi _ { B } =- 0.125 + 0.25 = 0.125 \mathrm { Nm } ^ { 2 } \mathrm { C } ^ { - 1 }{/tex}
  2. Using Gauss's law,
    {tex}\phi = \oint \mathbf { E } \cdot d \mathbf { l } = \frac { q } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \Rightarrow \ 0.125= \frac { q } { 8.85 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad q =8.85 \times 0.125 \times 10 ^ { - 12 }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad q = 1.1 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \mathrm { C }{/tex}
    So, the charge enclosed by the cylinder is {tex}1.1 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \mathrm { C }{/tex}.

question_text: Two large, thin metal plates are parallel and close to each other. On their inner faces, the plates have surface charge densities of opposite signs and of magnitude 17.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -22 C/m 2 . What is E: in the outer region of the first plate, in the outer region of the second plate, and between the plates?
explanation: The situation is represented in the following figure. A and B are two parallel plates close to each other. The outer region of plate A is labelled as I, outer region of plate B is labelled as III, and the region between the plates, A and B, is labelled as II. For region 1 E1 = E B - E A E= {tex}\sigma/2\in{/tex} o due to opposite charge densities E A is equal to zero, Similarly E B is equal to zero Electric field E in region II is given by the relation, E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\epsilon_o{/tex} = Permittivity of free space = 8.854 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 -12 N -1 C 2 m -2 E = {tex}\frac{17.0 \times 10^{-22}}{8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{/tex} = 1.92 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 -10 N/C Therefore, the electric field between the plates is 1.92 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 -10 N/C.

  2. At what distance from charge + Q1 on the line joining the two charges (in terms of Q1, Q2 and r) will this work done be zero.
question_text: Two point charges + Q 1 and - Q 2 are placed r distance apart. Obtain the expression for the amount of work done to place a third charge Q 3 at the midpoint of the line joining the two charges. At what distance from charge + Q 1 on the line joining the two charges (in terms of Q 1 , Q 2 and r) will this work done be zero.
explanation: Work done in placing the charge Q 3 at the mid point of line joining the charges = Q 3 {tex}\times{/tex} net potential at the mid point = {tex}\mathrm{Q}_3\left[\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}_1}{\frac {r}{2}}\right)+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{-\mathrm{Q}_2}{\frac {r}{2}}\right)\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 \mathrm{Q}_3}{r}\left[\mathrm{Q}_1-\mathrm{Q}_2\right]{/tex} Let the required distance be x, to the right of Q1. We then have net potential at this point = zero {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{\mathrm{Q}_1}{x}-\frac{\mathrm{Q}_2}{(x-r)}\right]{/tex} = 0 (x - r)Q 1 = Q 2 x x = {tex}\frac{r}{\left(\mathrm{Q}_1-\mathrm{Q}_2\right)} \mathrm{Q}_1{/tex}

We then have net potential at this point = zero {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{\mathrm{Q}_1}{x}-\frac{\mathrm{Q}_2}{(x-r)}\right]{/tex} = 0 (x - r)Q 1 = Q 2 x x = {tex}\frac{r}{\left(\mathrm{Q}_1-\mathrm{Q}_2\right)} \mathrm{Q}_1{/tex}

1. Work done in placing the charge Q3 at the mid point of line joining the charges
    = Q3{tex}\times{/tex}net potential at the mid point
    = {tex}\mathrm{Q}_3\left[\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{\mathrm{Q}_1}{\frac {r}{2}}\right)+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{-\mathrm{Q}_2}{\frac {r}{2}}\right)\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 \mathrm{Q}_3}{r}\left[\mathrm{Q}_1-\mathrm{Q}_2\right]{/tex}

An electron falls through a distance of 1.5 cm in a uniform electric field of magnitude 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 N/C

Calculate the time it takes to fall through this distance starting from rest.

If the direction of the field is reversed (fig. b) keeping its magnitude unchanged, calculate the time taken by a proton to fall through this distance starting from rest.
question_text: An electron falls through a distance of 1.5 cm in a uniform electric field of magnitude 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 N/C Calculate the time it takes to fall through this distance starting from rest. If the direction of the field is reversed (fig. b) keeping its magnitude unchanged, calculate the time taken by a proton to fall through this distance starting from rest.
explanation: For the electron The field is upward , so the negatively charged particle, electron experiences a downward froce of magnitude eE where E is the magnitude of the electric field. The acceleration of the electron is ae = eE/m Starting from rest , the time required by the electron to fall through a distance h {tex}a=\frac{e E}{m}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}t=\sqrt{\frac{3 \times 10^{-2} \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2.0 \times 10^{4}}}{/tex} = 2.92 * 10 -9 s for proton The field is downward, and the positively charged proton experiences a downward force of magnitude eE . The acceleration of the proton is {tex}t=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 1.67 \times 10^{-27}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2 \times 10^{4}}}{/tex} t = 1.3*10 -7

question_text: Define electric flux. Write its SI unit. Using Gauss' law, prove that the electric field at a point due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet is independent of distance from it. How is the field directed if the sheet is positively charged? negatively charged?
explanation: Electric flux is defined as the number of electric field lines passing through an area normal to the surface. Alternatively, surface integral of the electric field is defined as the electric flux through a closed surface {tex}\phi=\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}{/tex} SI unit: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{N} \cdot \mathrm{m}^{2}}{\mathrm{C}}{/tex} or volt.metre Outward flux through the Gaussian surface, {tex}2 \mathrm{EA}=\sigma \mathrm{A} / \varepsilon_{0}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm{E}=\sigma / 2 \varepsilon_{0}{/tex} In vector form, {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \hat{n}{/tex} where {tex}\hat{n}{/tex} is a unit vector normal to the plane, away from it. Hence, electric field is independent of the distance from the sheet. For positively charged sheet, the electric field is directed away from the sheet. For negatively charged sheet, the electric field is directed towards the plane of sheet.

question_text: Define electric flux. Is it a scalar or a vector quantity? A point charge {tex}q{/tex} is kept at a distance of {tex}\frac{d}{2}{/tex} directly above the centre of a square of side d. If the point charge is now moved to a point at a distance ' {tex}d{/tex} ' from the centre of the square and the side of the square is doubled, explain how the electric flux through the square will be affected.
explanation: Electric flux: The electric flux may be defined as the number of electric lines of force crossing through a surface normal to the surface. It can be measured as the surface integral of the electric field over that surface, i.e. {tex}\phi=\int_{s} \overline{E} \cdot d \overline{s}{/tex} Electric flux {tex}\phi{/tex} is a scalar quantity. Now to calculate the electric flux passing through the square of side d, draw a cube of side d such that it completely encloses the charge q. Now by using Gauss's law. Total flux through the all the six surfaces of a cube is given as {tex}\phi_{\text { total }}{/tex} = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\phi_{\text { square face }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\text { total charge enclosed }}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}6 \phi_{\text { square }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{\epsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\phi_{\text { square face }}=\frac{q}{6 \epsilon_{0}}{/tex} Hence the flux through the square of side d with charge q at a distance d/2 directly above the head is q/6ϵ o . If a charge is now moved to the distance d from the center of square and side of the square is doubled, then electric flux remains unchanged because electric flux in a closed surface depends only on the amount of charge contained inside the closed surface and is independent of the distance of charge.

question_text: Using Gauss's law in electrostatics, deduce an expression for electric field intensity due to a uniformly charged infinite plane sheet. If another identical sheet is placed parallel to it, show that there is no electric field in the region between the two sheets.
explanation: In the figure, if a is uniform surface charge density of an infinite plane sheet with x-axis to be normal to the plane, then by symmetry, the electric field will not depend on y and z coordinates and their directions. Considering the Gaussian rectangular parallelepiped surface with cross-sectional area A, two faces 1 and 2 will contribute to flux where electric field lines result as parallel to other faces which do not contribute to total flux. It is analyzed that the unit vector which is normal to the first surface will be in the negative x-direction while the unit vector which is normal to the second surface will be in positive +x direction, so flux {tex}E . \Delta S{/tex} by the surfaces will results equal and will add up. Let P be the point on which net flux through Gaussian surface = 2EA charge enclosed by closed surface = {tex}\sigma{/tex} A As per Gauss's law, {tex}\oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}2 E A=\frac{\sigma A}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} or {tex}E=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} This shows that the electric field around an infinite plane of charge does not vary with distance from the plane. In terms of vector, {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_{0}} \hat{n}{/tex} where, {tex}\hat n{/tex} = unit vector normal to plane and going away from it {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} = directed away from the plate if {tex}\sigma{/tex} is (+) positive and toward the plate if {tex}\sigma{/tex} is (-) negative. question_html: A charge is distributed uniformly over a ring of radius a. Obtain an expression for the electric field intensity E at a point on the axis of the ring. Hence, show that for points at large distances from the ring, it behaves like a point charge.
question_text: A charge is distributed uniformly over a ring of radius a . Obtain an expression for the electric field intensity E at a point on the axis of the ring. Hence, show that for points at large distances from the ring, it behaves like a point charge.
explanation: From the given figure it is clear that net Electric Field at point P {tex}=\int_{0}^{2 \pi a} d E \cos \theta{/tex} dE = Electric field due to a small element having charge dq {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{d q}{r^{2}}{/tex} Let {tex}\lambda{/tex} = Linear charge density {tex}=\frac{d q}{d l}{/tex} dq = {tex}\lambda{/tex} dl Hence E {tex}=\int_{0}^{2 \pi a} \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\lambda d l}{r^{2}} \times \frac{x}{r}{/tex} , where {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{x}{r}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\lambda x}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{3}}(2 \pi a){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Q x}{\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} , where total charge {tex}Q=\lambda \times 2 \pi a{/tex} At large distance i.e., x >> a {tex}\mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{Q}}{x^{2}}{/tex} This is the Electric field due to a point charge at distance x.
solution: From the given figure it is clear that net Electric Field at point P {tex}=\int_{0}^{2 \pi a} d E \cos \theta{/tex} dE = Electric field due to a small element having charge dq {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{d q}{r^{2}}{/tex} Let {tex}\lambda{/tex} = Linear charge density {tex}=\frac{d q}{d l}{/tex} dq = {tex}\lambda{/tex} dl Hence E {tex}=\int_{0}^{2 \pi a} \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\lambda d l}{r^{2}} \times \frac{x}{r}{/tex} , where {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{x}{r}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\lambda x}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{3}}(2 \pi a){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Q x}{\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex} , where total charge {tex}Q=\lambda \times 2 \pi a{/tex} At large distance i.e., x >> a {tex}\mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{Q}}{x^{2}}{/tex} This is the Electric field due to a point charge at distance x.
solution_html:
From the given figure it is clear that net Electric Field at point P {tex}=\int_{0}^{2 \pi a} d E \cos \theta{/tex}
dE = Electric field due to a small element having charge dq
{tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{d q}{r^{2}}{/tex}
Let {tex}\lambda{/tex} = Linear charge density
{tex}=\frac{d q}{d l}{/tex}
dq = {tex}\lambda{/tex}dl
Hence E {tex}=\int_{0}^{2 \pi a} \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\lambda d l}{r^{2}} \times \frac{x}{r}{/tex}, where {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{x}{r}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\lambda x}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{3}}(2 \pi a){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Q x}{\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{/tex},
where total charge {tex}Q=\lambda \times 2 \pi a{/tex}
At large distance i.e., x >> a
{tex}\mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{Q}}{x^{2}}{/tex}
This is the Electric field due to a point charge at distance x.

question_html: State Gauss's law in electrostatics. Use this law to derive an expression for the electric field due to an infinitely long straight wire of linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} Cm-1.
question_text: State Gauss's law in electrostatics. Use this law to derive an expression for the electric field due to an infinitely long straight wire of linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} Cm -1 .
explanation: Gauss's law in electrostatics: It states that total electric flux over the closed surface S in vacuum is {tex}\frac{1}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} times the total charge (q) contained in side S. {tex}\therefore {\phi _E} = \oint\limits_S {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {dS} = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} Electric field due to an infinitely long straight wire: Let an infinitely long line charge having linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} . Assume a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius r and length l coaxial with the line charge to determine its electric field at distance r. By symmetry, the electric field E has same magnitude at each point of the curved surface S 1 and is directed radially outward. So angle at surfaces between {tex}\overrightarrow {dS}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow E{/tex} is zero, and angle of {tex}{\overrightarrow {dS} _2},\,{\overrightarrow {dS} _3}{/tex} with {tex}\overrightarrow E {/tex} at S 2 and S 3 are 90°. Total flux through the cylindrical surface, {tex}\oint {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {ds} = \oint\limits_{{S_1}} {\overrightarrow E } \cdot {\overrightarrow {dS} _1} + \oint\limits_{{S_2}} {\overrightarrow E } \cdot {\overrightarrow {dS} _2} + \oint\limits_{{S_3}} {\overrightarrow E } \cdot {\overrightarrow {dS} _3}{/tex} {tex} = \oint\limits_{{S_1}} {E\,d{S_1}} \cdot \cos 0^\circ + \oint\limits_{{S_2}} {E\,d{S_2}} \cdot \cos 90^\circ{/tex} {tex} + \oint\limits_{{S_3}} {E\,d{S_3}} \cdot \cos 90^\circ {/tex} {tex} = E\oint {d{S_1}} = E \times 2\pi rl{/tex} Since {tex}\lambda{/tex} is the charge per unit length and l is the length of the wire, Thus, the charge enclosed {tex}q = \lambda l{/tex} According to Gaussian law, {tex}\oint\limits_S {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {dS} = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} or, {tex}E \times 2\pi rl = \frac{{\lambda l}}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore E = \frac{\lambda }{{2\pi {\varepsilon _0}r}}{/tex}

Assume a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius r and length l coaxial with the line charge to determine its electric field at distance r. By symmetry, the electric field E has same magnitude at each point of the curved surface S 1 and is directed radially outward. So angle at surfaces between {tex}\overrightarrow {dS}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow E{/tex} is zero, and angle of {tex}{\overrightarrow {dS} _2},\,{\overrightarrow {dS} _3}{/tex} with {tex}\overrightarrow E {/tex} at S 2 and S 3 are 90°. Total flux through the cylindrical surface, {tex}\oint {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {ds} = \oint\limits_{{S_1}} {\overrightarrow E } \cdot {\overrightarrow {dS} _1} + \oint\limits_{{S_2}} {\overrightarrow E } \cdot {\overrightarrow {dS} _2} + \oint\limits_{{S_3}} {\overrightarrow E } \cdot {\overrightarrow {dS} _3}{/tex} {tex} = \oint\limits_{{S_1}} {E\,d{S_1}} \cdot \cos 0^\circ + \oint\limits_{{S_2}} {E\,d{S_2}} \cdot \cos 90^\circ{/tex} {tex} + \oint\limits_{{S_3}} {E\,d{S_3}} \cdot \cos 90^\circ {/tex} {tex} = E\oint {d{S_1}} = E \times 2\pi rl{/tex} Since {tex}\lambda{/tex} is the charge per unit length and l is the length of the wire, Thus, the charge enclosed {tex}q = \lambda l{/tex} According to Gaussian law, {tex}\oint\limits_S {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {dS} = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} or, {tex}E \times 2\pi rl = \frac{{\lambda l}}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore E = \frac{\lambda }{{2\pi {\varepsilon _0}r}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Gauss's law in electrostatics: It states that total electric flux over the closed surface S in vacuum is {tex}\frac{1}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} times the total charge (q) contained in side S.
{tex}\therefore {\phi _E} = \oint\limits_S {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {dS} = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex}
Electric field due to an infinitely long straight wire:
Let an infinitely long line charge having linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex}. Assume a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius r and length l coaxial with the line charge to determine its electric field at distance r.
By symmetry, the electric field E has same magnitude at each point of the curved surface S1 and is directed radially outward. So angle at surfaces between {tex}\overrightarrow {dS}{/tex}  and {tex}\overrightarrow E{/tex} is zero, and angle of {tex}{\overrightarrow {dS} _2},\,{\overrightarrow {dS} _3}{/tex}  with {tex}\overrightarrow E {/tex} at S2 and S3 are 90°.

Total flux through the cylindrical surface,
{tex}\oint {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {ds} = \oint\limits_{{S_1}} {\overrightarrow E } \cdot {\overrightarrow {dS} _1} + \oint\limits_{{S_2}} {\overrightarrow E } \cdot {\overrightarrow {dS} _2} + \oint\limits_{{S_3}} {\overrightarrow E } \cdot {\overrightarrow {dS} _3}{/tex}
{tex} = \oint\limits_{{S_1}} {E\,d{S_1}} \cdot \cos 0^\circ + \oint\limits_{{S_2}} {E\,d{S_2}} \cdot \cos 90^\circ{/tex} {tex} + \oint\limits_{{S_3}} {E\,d{S_3}} \cdot \cos 90^\circ {/tex}
{tex} = E\oint {d{S_1}} = E \times 2\pi rl{/tex}
Since {tex}\lambda{/tex} is the charge per unit length and l is the length of the wire,
Thus, the charge enclosed
{tex}q = \lambda l{/tex}
According to Gaussian law,
{tex}\oint\limits_S {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {dS} = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex}
or, {tex}E \times 2\pi rl = \frac{{\lambda l}}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore E = \frac{\lambda }{{2\pi {\varepsilon _0}r}}{/tex}

question_text: Define the term electric field intensity. Write its SI unit. Derive an expression for the electric field intensity at a point on the axis of an electric dipole.
explanation: The force experienced by a unit positive charge placed at a point is termed as the electric field intensity at that point. The force experienced by a unit positive charge placed at a point is termed as the electric field intensity at that point. It is vector quantity it's direction is in the force acting on +ve charge.
The SI unit of electric field intensity is NC-1
Electric field at an axial point of electric dipole:
Assume point P is located at distance r from the centre of an electric dipole as shown in the figure.

The electric field at point P is {tex}\overrightarrow E = {\overrightarrow E _{ + q}} + {\overrightarrow E _{ - q}}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow E = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\left[ {\frac{q}{{{{(r - a)}^2}}} - \frac{q}{{{{(r + a)}^2}}}} \right]\widehat p{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow E = \frac{q}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\left[ {\frac{{{{(r + a)}^2} - {{(r - a)}^2}}}{{({r^2} - {a^2})^2}}} \right]\widehat p{/tex}
or {tex}\overrightarrow E = \frac{q}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\left[ {\frac{{4ra}}{{{{({r^2} - {a^2})}^2}}}} \right]\widehat p{/tex}

or {tex}\overrightarrow E = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{2p}}{{{r^3}}}{/tex} [ For short dipole i.e.,  r > > a ]

question_html:
1. Consider two identical point charges located at points (0, 0) and (a, 0).
    1. Is there a point on the line joining them at which the electric field is zero?
    2. Is there a point on the line joining them at which the electric potential is zero?
      Justify your answers for each case.
  2. State the significance of negative value of electrostatic potential energy of a system of charges.
    Three charges are placed at the corners of an equilateral triangle ABC of side 2.0 m as shown in figure. Calculate the electric potential energy of the system of three charges.
question_text: Consider two identical point charges located at points (0, 0) and (a, 0). Is there a point on the line joining them at which the electric field is zero? Is there a point on the line joining them at which the electric potential is zero? Justify your answers for each case. State the significance of negative value of electrostatic potential energy of a system of charges. Three charges are placed at the corners of an equilateral triangle ABC of side 2.0 m as shown in figure. Calculate the electric potential energy of the system of three charges.
explanation: Yes, electric field is zero at mid point. Electric field being a vector quantity, its resultant is zero. No, potential cannot be zero on line joining the charges. Electric potential being a scalar quantity, the net potential due to two identical charges cannot be zero. Negative value of electrostatic potential energy of a system signifies that the system has attractive forces. {tex}U=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \times \frac{q_1 q_2}{r}{/tex} {tex}U=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_0}\left[\frac{q_A q_B}{r}+\frac{q_B q_c}{r}+\frac{q_c q_A}{r}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9}{2}[-16-8+8] \times 10^{-12}{/tex} = -7.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 J

Electric potential being a scalar quantity, the net potential due to two identical charges cannot be zero. Negative value of electrostatic potential energy of a system signifies that the system has attractive forces. {tex}U=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \times \frac{q_1 q_2}{r}{/tex} {tex}U=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_0}\left[\frac{q_A q_B}{r}+\frac{q_B q_c}{r}+\frac{q_c q_A}{r}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9}{2}[-16-8+8] \times 10^{-12}{/tex} = -7.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 J
solution_html:
1. Yes, electric field is zero at mid point.
      Electric field being a vector quantity, its resultant is zero.
    2. No, potential cannot be zero on line joining the charges.
      Electric potential being a scalar quantity, the net potential due to two identical charges cannot be zero.
  2. Negative value of electrostatic potential energy of a system signifies that the system has attractive forces.

    {tex}U=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \times \frac{q_1 q_2}{r}{/tex}
    {tex}U=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_0}\left[\frac{q_A q_B}{r}+\frac{q_B q_c}{r}+\frac{q_c q_A}{r}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^9}{2}[-16-8+8] \times 10^{-12}{/tex}
    = -7.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 J

explanation: Gauss's law in electrostatics: It states that total electric flux over the closed surface S in vacuum is {tex}\frac{1}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} times the total charge (q) contained in side S. {tex}\therefore {\phi _E} = \oint\limits_S {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {dS} = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} Let an infinitely long line charge having linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} . Assume a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius r and length l coaxial with the line charge to determine its electric field at distance r. Total flux through the cylindrical surface, {tex}\oint {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {ds} = \oint\limits_{{S_1}} {\overrightarrow E } \cdot {\overrightarrow {dS} _1} + \oint\limits_{{S_2}} {\overrightarrow E } \cdot {\overrightarrow {dS} _2} + \oint\limits_{{S_3}} {\overrightarrow E } \cdot {\overrightarrow {dS} _3}{/tex} {tex} = \oint\limits_{{S_1}} {E\,d{S_1}} \cdot \cos 0^\circ + \oint\limits_{{S_2}} {E\,d{S_2}} \cdot \cos 90^\circ{/tex} {tex} + \oint\limits_{{S_3}} {E\,d{S_3}} \cdot \cos 90^\circ {/tex} {tex} = E\oint {d{S_1}} = E \times 2\pi rl{/tex} Since {tex}\lambda{/tex} is the charge per unit length and l is the length of the wire, Thus, the charge enclosed {tex}q = \lambda l{/tex} According to Gaussian law, {tex}\oint\limits_S {\overrightarrow E } \cdot \overrightarrow {dS} = \frac{q}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} or, {tex}E \times 2\pi rl = \frac{{\lambda l}}{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore E = \frac{\lambda }{{2\pi {\varepsilon _0}r}}{/tex} Electric field intensity at a distance r from line charge of density {tex}\lambda {/tex} is {tex}E = \frac{\lambda }{{2\pi {\varepsilon _0}r}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Field intensity on negative charge (r = 0.02 m) {tex}{E_1} = \frac{{4 \times {{10}^{ - 4}} \times 9 \times {{10}^9} \times 2}}{{0.02}} = 3.6 \times {10^8}N/C{/tex} Force on negative charge {tex}{F_1} = q{E_1} = 2 \times {10^{ - 8}}\left( {3.6 \times {{10}^8}} \right) = 7.2N{/tex} It is directed towards the line charge. Similarly field intensity at positive charge (r = 0.022 m) {tex}{E_2} = \frac{{4 \times {{10}^{ - 4}} \times 9 \times {{10}^9} \times 2}}{{0.022}} = 3.27 \times {10^8}N/C{/tex} Force on positive charge {tex}{F_2} = q{E_2} = 2 \times {10^{ - 8}}(3.27 \times {10^8}) = 6.54N{/tex} It is directed away from the line charge. {tex}\therefore {/tex} Net force on the dipole, F = F 1 - F 2 = (7.2 - 6.54)N = 0.66 N F is towards the line charge.

    1. at the centre of the dipole ({tex}{r}=0{/tex}), and

      where 2 a is the length of the dipole.
  2. An electric field {tex}\vec{E}=(10 x+5) \hat{i} N / C{/tex} exists in a region in which a cube of side L is kept as shown in the figure. Here x and {tex}L{/tex} are in metres. Calculate the net flux through the cube.
question_text: Obtain an expression for the electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} due to a dipole of dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} at a point on its equatorial plane and specify its direction. explanation: The magnitudes of the electric field due to two charges {tex}+q{/tex} and {tex}-q{/tex} are {tex}E_{+q}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{1}{\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}E_{-q}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{1}{\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)}{/tex} The total electric field {tex}\vec{E}=-\left(E_{+q}+E_{-q}\right) \cos \theta \hat{p}{/tex} {tex}\vec{E}=-\frac{\vec{p}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\left(r^{2}+a^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} Direction of electric field is opposite to dipole moment {tex}(\vec{p}){/tex} At centre of dipole, {tex}{r}=0{/tex} {tex}\vec{E}=-\frac{-\vec{p}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} a^{3}}{/tex} At a point {tex}{r} \gg {a}{/tex} {tex}\vec{E}=-\frac{-\vec{p}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^3}{/tex} {tex}\vec{E}= (10 x+5) \hat{i} {~N} / {C}{/tex} {tex}\phi_{L}=\int \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d s}{/tex} {tex}=-E_{L}\left(L^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=-5 L^{2}{/tex} {tex}\phi_{R}= E_{R}\left(L^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}= (10 L+5) L^{2}{/tex} {tex}\phi_{\text {net }}= \phi_{L}+\phi_{R}{/tex} {tex}= -5 L^{2}+(10 L+5) L^{2}{/tex} {tex}= 10 L^{3} {Nm}^{2} / {C}{/tex}

    {tex}=-5 L^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\phi_{R}= E_{R}\left(L^{2}\right){/tex}
    {tex}= (10 L+5) L^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\phi_{\text {net }}= \phi_{L}+\phi_{R}{/tex}
    {tex}= -5 L^{2}+(10 L+5) L^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}= 10 L^{3} {Nm}^{2} / {C}{/tex}

question_text: State coulomb's law of force in electrostatics. Express it in vector form. A {tex}12\mu C{/tex} charge is placed at the distance of 10 cm from a linear charge of {tex}100\mu C{/tex} uniformly distributed once the length of 10 cm as shown in figure. Find the force on {tex}12\mu C{/tex} charge.
explanation: Columb's law states that two point charges attract or repel each other with a force which is directly proportional to the product of the magnitudes of the charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. Consider that two like charges q 1 and q 2 are present at points A and B in vacuum at a distance r apart. The two charges will exert equal repulsive forces on each other. Let {tex}\overrightarrow {{F_{12}}} {/tex} be the force on charge q 1 due to charge q 2 and {tex}\overrightarrow {{F_{21}}} {/tex} be the force charge q 2 due to charge q 1 as shown in fig. According to Coulomb's law, the magnitude of force on charge q 2 due to q 1 is given by {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow {{F_{12}}} } \right| = \left| {\overrightarrow {{F_{21}}} } \right| = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{r^2}}}{/tex} ..... (1) Let {tex}{\hat r_{12}}{/tex} be a unit vector pointing from charge q 1 to q 2 and {tex}{\hat r_{21}}{/tex} , a unit vector pointing from charge q 2 to q 1 . As the force vector {tex}\overrightarrow {{F_{12}}} {/tex} is along the direction of unit vector {tex}\overrightarrow {{F_{21}}} {/tex} is along the direction of unit vector {tex}{\hat r_{21}}{/tex} , we have {tex}\overrightarrow {{F_{12}}} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{r^2}}}{\hat r_{21}}{/tex} ..... (2) Also, as the force vector {tex}\overrightarrow {{F_{12}}} {/tex} is along the direction of unit vector {tex}{\hat r_{21}}{/tex} , it follows that {tex}\overrightarrow {{F_{21}}} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{r^2}}}{\hat r_{12}}{/tex} The equation (1) and (2) express Columb's law in vector form. Force on {tex}12\mu C{/tex} charge due to an elementary part of the linear charge, {tex}dF = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{(\lambda dx)12 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = 9 \times {10^9} \times \frac{{100 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{10 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}} \times 12 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times \frac{{dx}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = 1.08\frac{{dx}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} Net force on {tex}12\mu C{/tex} charge {tex}F = \int {dF} = 1.08{/tex} {tex}\int\limits_{0.10}^{0.20} {\frac{{dx}}{{{x^2}}}} = 1.08\left[ { - \frac{1}{x}} \right]_{0.01}^{0.20}{/tex} {tex} = 1.08\left[ {\frac{1}{{0.10}} - \frac{1}{{0.20}}} \right] = 10.8\left[ {1 - \frac{1}{2}} \right] = 5.4N{/tex}

1. Columb's law states that two point charges attract or repel each other with a force which is directly proportional to the product of the magnitudes of the charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
    Consider that two like charges q1 and q2 are present at points A and B in vacuum at a distance r apart. The two charges will exert equal repulsive forces on each other. Let {tex}\overrightarrow {{F_{12}}} {/tex} be the force on charge q1 due to charge q2 and {tex}\overrightarrow {{F_{21}}} {/tex} be the force charge q2 due to charge q1 as shown in fig.

    According to Coulomb's law, the magnitude of force on charge q2 due to q1 is given by
    {tex}\left| {\overrightarrow {{F_{12}}} } \right| = \left| {\overrightarrow {{F_{21}}} } \right| = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{r^2}}}{/tex} ..... (1)
    Let {tex}{\hat r_{12}}{/tex} be a unit vector pointing from charge q1 to q2 and {tex}{\hat r_{21}}{/tex}, a unit vector pointing from charge q2 to q1. As the force vector {tex}\overrightarrow {{F_{12}}} {/tex} is along the direction of unit vector {tex}\overrightarrow {{F_{21}}} {/tex} is along the direction of unit vector {tex}{\hat r_{21}}{/tex}, we have
    {tex}\overrightarrow {{F_{12}}} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{r^2}}}{\hat r_{21}}{/tex} ..... (2)
    Also, as the force vector {tex}\overrightarrow {{F_{12}}} {/tex} is along the direction of unit vector {tex}{\hat r_{21}}{/tex}, it follows that
    {tex}\overrightarrow {{F_{21}}} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{r^2}}}{\hat r_{12}}{/tex}
    The equation (1) and (2) express Columb's law in vector form.
  2. Force on {tex}12\mu C{/tex} charge due to an elementary part of the linear charge,
    {tex}dF = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} \cdot \frac{{(\lambda dx)12 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 9 \times {10^9} \times \frac{{100 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{10 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}} \times 12 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times \frac{{dx}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 1.08\frac{{dx}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex}

    Net force on {tex}12\mu C{/tex} charge
    {tex}F = \int {dF} = 1.08{/tex} {tex}\int\limits_{0.10}^{0.20} {\frac{{dx}}{{{x^2}}}} = 1.08\left[ { - \frac{1}{x}} \right]_{0.01}^{0.20}{/tex}
    {tex} = 1.08\left[ {\frac{1}{{0.10}} - \frac{1}{{0.20}}} \right] = 10.8\left[ {1 - \frac{1}{2}} \right] = 5.4N{/tex}
